3. Naive Bayes model using TF-IDF Vectors for Unigram+Bigram¶

Config of the model trained

Train Time = 0.13 seconds

Training Data Check

Confusion Matrix Built - Yes

F1 Score for Positive = 0.93

F1 Score for Negative = 0.85

AUC plotted - Yes

AUC = 0.90

Accuracy computed - Yes

Accuracy = 0.88

Feature Engg

Categorical/Numerical Features Added - Yes

Name 2 features added - sentence length,punctuation count

Cross Validation - Yes (0.84)

Interpretability

Interpretability Implemented? - Yes, Local

2 interesting findings?

-Over reliance on one word (like) and ignoring other words like good , fresh , nice and tasty

-Capturing nuanced meaning of words such as though and would.

Testing Data Check

Confusion Matrix Built - Yes

F1 Score for Positive = 0.90

F1 Score for Negative = 0.79

AUC plotted - Yes

AUC = 0.91

Accuracy computed - Yes

Accuracy = 0.83

Prompts Used :

  1. Start : Naive Bayes model using TF-IDF Vectors for Unigram+Bigram

  2. End : what is hstack in the code doing

Importing Libraries

Data Preprocessing

In [375]:
def convert_stars_to_sentiment(stars):
    if stars <= 2:
        return 0  # Negative
    elif stars == 3:
        return 1  # Neutral
    else:
        return 2  # Positive
In [376]:
df['sentiment'] = df['stars'].apply(convert_stars_to_sentiment)

Data Cleaning

In [378]:
def preprocess_text(text):
    text = text.lower()
    text = re.sub(r'http\S+|www\S+', '', text) #For Removing URL
    text = re.sub(r'@\w+', '', text ) #For Removing Mentions
    text = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z\s]', '', text) #For removing special characters
    tokens = word_tokenize(text)
    lemmatizer = WordNetLemmatizer()
    tokens = [lemmatizer.lemmatize(token) for token in tokens]
    stop_words = set(stopwords.words('english'))
    tokens = [token for token in tokens if token not in stop_words]
    return ' '.join(tokens)
In [379]:
df['clean_text'] = df['text'].apply(preprocess_text)

Custom Feature Engineering Function

In [382]:
def get_sentence_length(text):
    return len(text.split())

def get_word_density(text):
    words = text.split()
    return len(set(words)) / len(words) if len(words) > 0 else 0

def count_punctuation(text):
    return sum(1 for char in text if char in ".,!?")

def count_stopwords(text):
    return sum(1 for word in text.split() if word in stopwords.words('english'))
In [383]:
# Apply feature engineering
df['sentence_length'] = df['clean_text'].apply(get_sentence_length)
df['word_density'] = df['clean_text'].apply(get_word_density)
df['punctuation_count'] = df['clean_text'].apply(count_punctuation)
df['stopword_count'] = df['clean_text'].apply(count_stopwords)
In [384]:
X_features = df[['sentence_length', 'word_density', 'punctuation_count', 'stopword_count']].values
In [385]:
y = np.array(df['sentiment'])
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(df['clean_text'], y, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)
In [386]:
vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(
    ngram_range=(1, 2),  # Unigrams & Bigrams
    max_features=10000,   # Limit vocabulary
    min_df=3,            # Ignore rare words
    sublinear_tf=True,   #scales the term frequency (TF) using a logarithmic function instead of using raw counts which helps to improve neatral class improvement.
    max_df=0.9
)
X_train_tfidf = vectorizer.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_tfidf = vectorizer.transform(X_test)
In [387]:
start_time = time.time()
In [388]:
nb_model = MultinomialNB(alpha= 0.1)
nb_model.fit(X_train_tfidf, y_train)
Out[388]:
MultinomialNB(alpha=0.1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
MultinomialNB(alpha=0.1)
In [389]:
y_train_pred = nb_model.predict(X_train_tfidf)

# Testing Accuracy
y_test_pred = nb_model.predict(X_test_tfidf)
y_train_prob = nb_model.predict_proba(X_train_tfidf)
y_prob = nb_model.predict_proba(X_test_tfidf)
In [390]:
#  Compute Evaluation Metrics
train_accuracy = accuracy_score(y_train, y_train_pred)
test_accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_test_pred)
precision = precision_score(y_test, y_test_pred, average='weighted')
recall = recall_score(y_test, y_test_pred, average='weighted')
report = classification_report(y_test, y_test_pred, output_dict=True)
f1_positive_train = f1_score(y_train, y_train_pred, average=None)[2]  # F1-score for Positive (label=2)
f1_positive = f1_score(y_test, y_test_pred, average=None)[2]  # F1-score for Positive (label=2)
f1_negative_train = f1_score(y_train, y_train_pred, average=None)[0]  # F1-score for Negative (label=0)
f1_negative = f1_score(y_test, y_test_pred, average=None)[0]
y_train_bin = label_binarize(y_train, classes=[0, 1, 2])
y_test_bin = label_binarize(y_test, classes=[0, 1, 2])  # Convert labels to binary for AUC
auc_score_train = roc_auc_score(y_train, y_train_prob, multi_class='ovr')
auc_score = roc_auc_score(y_test_bin, y_prob, multi_class='ovr')
In [391]:
#  End training time measurement
end_time = time.time()
training_time = end_time - start_time
In [392]:
# Print Results
print(f" Naïve Bayes Training Time: {training_time:.2f} seconds")
print(f" Naïve Bayes training Accuracy: {train_accuracy:.2f}")
print(f" Naïve Bayes Accuracy: {test_accuracy:.2f}")
print(f" Naïve Bayes Precision: {precision:.2f}")
print(f" Naïve Bayes Recall: {recall:.2f}")
print(f" F1 Score (Positive_Train): {f1_positive_train:.2f}")
print(f" F1 Score (Positive): {f1_positive:.2f}")
print(f" F1 Score (Negative_Train): {f1_negative_train:.2f}")
print(f" F1 Score (Negative): {f1_negative:.2f}")
print(f" AUC Score_Train: {auc_score_train:.2f}")
print(f" AUC Score: {auc_score:.2f}")
print("\nNaïve Bayes Classification Report:\n", classification_report(y_test, y_test_pred))

AUC CURVE FOR TRAINING DATA

In [395]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
for i, label in enumerate(["Negative", "Neutral", "Positive"]):
    fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_train_bin[:, i], y_train_prob[:, i])
    plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f'{label} (AUC = {auc(fpr, tpr):.2f})')

plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--')  # Random Guess Line
plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("ROC Curve (Naïve Bayes) ")
plt.legend()
plt.grid()
plt.show()

AUC CURVE FOR TESTING DATA

In [396]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
for i, label in enumerate(["Negative", "Neutral", "Positive"]):
    fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin[:, i], y_prob[:, i])
    plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f'{label} (AUC = {auc(fpr, tpr):.2f})')

plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--')  # Random Guess Line
plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("ROC Curve (Naïve Bayes)")
plt.legend()
plt.grid()
plt.show()

nb_model.fit(X_train_resampled, y_train_resampled)

After Sampling, Neutral F1-score improved from 0.16 → 0.39, meaning class balancing helped. Precision improved (0.80 → 0.83), meaning fewer false positives.

Hyperparameter Tuning

In [262]:
param_grid = {'alpha': [0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0]}
In [263]:
nb_model = MultinomialNB(alpha= 0.1)
In [264]:
grid_search = GridSearchCV(nb_model, param_grid, cv=4, scoring='accuracy', verbose=1, n_jobs=-1)
In [265]:
start_time = time.time()
In [266]:
grid_search.fit(X_train_resampled, y_train_resampled)

Fitting 4 folds for each of 5 candidates, totalling 20 fits

best_nb = grid_search.best_estimator_
best_alpha = grid_search.best_params_['alpha']
In [269]:
y_train_pred = best_nb.predict(X_train_tfidf)

In [270]:
#  Compute Metrics
train_accuracy = accuracy_score(y_train, y_train_pred)
test_accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_test_pred)
precision = precision_score(y_test, y_test_pred, average='weighted')
recall = recall_score(y_test, y_test_pred, average='weighted')
report = classification_report(y_test, y_test_pred, output_dict=True)
# Extract F1-Scores for Negative (0) and Positive (2) Sentiments
f1_negative = report['0']['f1-score']
f1_positive = report['2']['f1-score']
# Compute Multi-Class AUC
y_test_bin = label_binarize(y_test, classes=[0, 1, 2])  # Convert labels to binary for AUC
auc_score = roc_auc_score(y_test_bin, y_prob, multi_class='ovr')

After Tuning the Model , No real improvement over the previous step (sampling effects still present).

Neutral AUC remains at 0.81, meaning hyperparameter tuning alone is not solving the issue.

2nd Time Tuning

In [228]:
train_accuracy = accuracy_score(y_train, y_train_pred)
test_accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_test_pred)
precision = precision_score(y_test, y_test_pred, average='weighted')
recall = recall_score(y_test, y_test_pred, average='weighted')
report = classification_report(y_test, y_test_pred, output_dict=True)
# Extract F1-Scores for Negative (0) and Positive (2) Sentiments
f1_negative = report['0']['f1-score']
f1_positive = report['2']['f1-score']
#  Compute Multi-Class AUC
y_test_bin = label_binarize(y_test, classes=[0, 1, 2])  # Convert labels to binary for AUC
auc_score = roc_auc_score(y_test_bin, y_prob, multi_class='ovr')

print(f"Naïve Bayes Recall: {recall:.2f}")

print(f" F1 Score (Positive Sentiment): {f1_positive:.2f}")
print(f" AUC Score: {auc_score:.2f}")

cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_test_pred)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Blues",
            xticklabels=["Negative", "Neutral", "Positive"],
            yticklabels=["Negative", "Neutral", "Positive"])
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.title("Confusion Matrix")
plt.show()
In [231]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
for i, label in enumerate(["Negative", "Neutral", "Positive"]):
    fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_test_bin[:, i], y_prob[:, i])
    plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f'{label} (AUC = {auc(fpr, tpr):.2f})')

plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--')  # Random Guess Line
plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("ROC Curve (Fine-Tuned Naïve Bayes)")
plt.legend()
plt.grid()
plt.show()

After tuning 2nd Time, Small improvement in test accuracy (0.79 → 0.80), indicating better generalization.

Neutral AUC dropped from 0.81 → 0.79, meaning the tuning may not be working for neutral cases.

STRATIFIED CROSS VALIDATION

In [243]:
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score
from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB

# Define the stratified k-fold cross-validation
kf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

# Initialize model (example: Naive Bayes)
model = MultinomialNB(alpha=1.0)

# Perform cross-validation
scores = cross_val_score(model, X_train_resampled, y_train_resampled, cv=kf, scoring='accuracy')

# Print cross-validation results
print(f"Cross_Validation Accuracy:  {scores.mean():.2f}")

Cross_Validation Accuracy:  0.84

In [245]:
import lime
from lime.lime_text import LimeTextExplainer
# Train the Naïve Bayes Model
model = MultinomialNB(alpha=0.1)
model.fit(X_train_resampled, y_train_resampled)

# Pick a sample from your dataset (e.g., the 10th test sample)
sample_index = 10
sample_text = df.iloc[sample_index]['text']
print(f"Sample Review: " , {sample_text})
explainer = LimeTextExplainer(class_names=['Negative', 'Neutral', 'Positive'])


# Define a wrapper for predict_proba to preprocess text
def predict_proba_wrapper(texts):
    # Vectorize the input text data
    texts_vectorized = vectorizer.transform(texts)
    # Return predictions from the model
    return model.predict_proba(texts_vectorized)

# Use the wrapper with LIME
exp = explainer.explain_instance(
    sample_text,
    predict_proba_wrapper,  # Use the wrapped function
    num_features=10
)

# Visualize the explanation
exp.show_in_notebook(text=True)

Sample Review:  {"I went here on the recommendation of the concierge at the hotel I was staying at. I didn't make a reservation as my plan was to show up a few minutes before 5pm and hope that they weren't busy and my plan paid off, as I got right in. I waited at the bar for about 10m as they got things ready and read my book. The lighting was low but I was still able to read my book. They have a large menu with a lot of seafood dishes. The first thing they bring out are cheddar biscuits, which are really tasty. I ordered the local flounder. It ended up being a very big dish.  All seafood is flown in that day so it's fresh. The flounder also had vegetables with it. I really liked the dish. Enough after the biscuits, that I was too full to order dessert, even though I would have liked some sorbet. I liked the extensive menu here and the waiter service was really good. The atmosphere was nice for chatting, (although I happened to be alone this time)."}

Model train_Accuracy test_Accuracy Precision Recall F1-Score (Negative) F1_Score (Neutral) F1-Score (Positive) Neg_AUC Neu_AUC Pos_AUC training_time
0 Naive Bayes (Initial) 0.88 0.83 0.80 0.83 0.79 0.16 0.90 0.95 0.84 0.94 0.13 Seconds
1 Naive Bayes (Sampling) 0.86 0.79 0.83 0.79 0.76 0.39 0.88 0.93 0.81 0.94 1.91 Seconds
2 Naive Bayes (1st Tuning) 0.86 0.79 0.83 0.79 0.76 0.39 0.88 0.93 0.81 0.94 4.48 Seconds
3 Naive Bayes (2nd Tuning) 0.87 0.80 0.83 0.80 0.76 0.38 0.89 0.93 0.79 0.94 2.59 Seconds

Final Take :

  1. The Initial Model performed the best overall, with the highest test accuracy(No Overfitting compare to other model) and best neutral AUC.

2.Further tuning did not bring significant benefits and even caused a drop in test performance.

  1. The hyperparameter tuning overfitted the training data without real performance gains.

Further we can try following things:

  1. Try Complement Naive Bayes
  2. Ensemble Naive Bayes with other model
  3. Use Word Embeddings(Word2Vec,FastText)
  4. Try Logistic Regression with L2 regularisation
In [ ]:
